explanation_md: `show(5)` passes an `int` literal, selecting `show(int)`, which prints `int`. `show(2.5)` passes a `double` literal, selecting `show(double)`, which prints `double`. The compiler picks the exact-match overload in each case.

prompt_md: Which of the following is a valid way to create two distinct overloads of a function named `compute`?

options.a: Define `int compute(int x)` and `double compute(int x)` — different return types
options.b: Define `void compute(int x)` and `void compute(const int x)` — const value parameter
options.c: Define `void compute(int x)` and `void compute(double x)` — different parameter types
options.d: Define `void compute(int x)` twice in the same translation unit

explanation_md: Overloads must differ in the number or types of their parameters. Option c changes the parameter type from `int` to `double`, which is a valid distinction. Return type alone (option a) does not differentiate overloads. `const` on a value parameter (option b) is ignored for overload purposes. Defining the same signature twice (option d) is a redefinition error.

`print(1)` has one argument, matching `print(int)` → prints `A`. `print(1, 2)` has two arguments, matching `print(int, int)` → prints `B`. Overloads can differ by the number of parameters, and the compiler counts arguments to select the right one.

prompt_md: A developer defines `void log(int x)` and `void log(double x)`, then calls `log('Z')`. What happens?

options.a: Compile error: no overload takes a `char`
options.b: The `int` overload is called because `char` promotes to `int`
options.c: The `double` overload is called because `char` converts to floating-point

explanation_md: When no exact-match overload exists, the compiler looks for the best implicit conversion. `char` undergoes integral promotion to `int`, making `log(int)` the best match. The `double` overload would require a standard conversion, which ranks lower than a promotion.

options.a: The second function uses `static_cast`, which is not allowed in an overload
options.b: Both functions have identical parameter lists; return type does not distinguish overloads
options.d: Two functions with the same name cannot have different return types under any circumstances

explanation_md: Overload resolution is decided before the compiler knows what to do with the return value, so the return type is not part of the overload signature. Both declarations have the parameter list `(int x)`, so they conflict. The fix is to change a parameter type or use different function names.
